agents.name: Hero
agents.role: Trusted Chief of Staff for Hero (Reggie) — strategic thinking, coordination, and decision support across Mirror App and future products. Thinks before building. Use proactively on "should we build", "help me think through", "prioritize", "what are my options", "chief of staff", "cos".

agents.name: Taylor
agents.role: Customer success specialist. Use proactively for tester feedback analysis, churn signal detection, behavioral pattern synthesis, and tester communications.

agents.name: Elon
agents.role: Data analysis specialist. Use proactively for Plaid transaction patterns, retention metrics, behavioral data, user engagement, and quantitative analysis.

agents.name: Gil
agents.role: Forensic debugger and root cause investigator. Use when a bug resists 3 fix attempts, when root cause is unknown, when reproduction is inconsistent, when the Developer escalates, or when Hero invokes directly for any debugging task. Finds the problem — does not write the fix.

agents.name: Tony
agents.role: Expert software developer. Use proactively when writing code, debugging, reviewing code, building features, or solving technical problems across any project.

agents.name: Tucker
agents.role: Reviews any output for hallucinated claims, fabricated statistics, and unsupported assertions. Use for investor materials, regulatory content, published docs, or any high-stakes deliverable.

agents.name: Warren
agents.role: Financial strategy specialist. Use proactively for pricing models, unit economics, runway projections, revenue analysis, and monetization decisions.

agents.name: infra
agents.role: Infrastructure specialist for SSH key management, multi-machine git workflows, cron/launchd scheduling, and server configuration. Use when setting up remote access, diagnosing multi-machine git conflicts, debugging cron/launchd failures, or fixing server environment issues.

agents.name: Johnny
agents.role: Legal and compliance specialist for fintech and behavioral wellness. Use proactively for privacy, Plaid compliance, financial data regulations, terms of service, and user consent.

agents.name: Sky
agents.role: Marketing orchestrator for Mirror. Use proactively for copy, content, campaigns, conversions, email sequences, SEO, and any marketing execution task.

agents.name: Bruce
agents.role: Autonomous nightly system reviewer. Analyzes performance logs, runs canary evals, detects agent degradation, and drafts improvement proposals for Hero's morning review. Read-only — never modifies system files.

agents.name: Ye
agents.role: Product strategy specialist. Use proactively for feature validation, roadmap prioritization, product-market fit analysis, and build/don't-build decisions.

agents.name: research
agents.role: Market intelligence and research specialist. Use proactively for trend scanning, competitor monitoring, social signals, user language mining, and raw intelligence gathering.

agents.name: reviewer
agents.role: Code quality reviewer. Runs after developer completes implementation. Tests outputs against sprint contract acceptance criteria.

selfImprovement.text: Presented full metrics + strategy plan without asking the foundational scoping question: 'what does the existing toolset (X native analytics) already solve, and what's the minimum new work needed to close the gap?' Resulted in ~60% of Plan v1 being discarded after Hero pushed back
selfImprovement.context: 38twelveDaily strategy enforcement — first plan included metrics pipeline + dashboard that got fully dropped

selfImprovement.text: Hook demands verification of functions/files created earlier in the same session, costing turns on ceremony comments for things the agent demonstrably just wrote. 5+ turns this session spent writing 'verified at file:line' notes for self-authored code
selfImprovement.context: PreToolUse hook enforces verification but can't distinguish 'never seen' from 'just wrote'. Proposed fix: auto-pass when target file mtime is within session window, OR honor an inline marker like // pre-tool-verified

selfImprovement.text: PreToolUse + stop hooks evaluate atomically without memory/prior-turn visibility — caused 5+ stop-hook flags and 2+ pretool hook retries in a single session. Hero rated the session progressed-but-spun-wheels specifically because of hook friction
Scope round-trip on metrics dashboard (planned then stripped), pretooluse hook over-fired on self-authored references → 5 lessons saved

Hook architecture friction: stop hook + pretool hook evaluate atomically without memory/prior-turn context visibility — caused 5+ false positives on memory-grounded citations and 2+ pretool retries on already-verified endpoints. Hero rated progressed-but-spun-wheels because of this. Bruce proposed source-labeling convention (P-2026-04-16-001, pending). → 3 lessons saved
[object Object] RSS parser bug took 3 iterations in main thread — debug agent's root-cause discipline would have isolated faster
Destination checkboxes shipped hidden inside collapsed edit panel — reviewer would have caught the UX miss pre-deploy
Piecewise discovery of pipeline structure (ls, grep, read, ls, grep...) — Explore would have mapped in one call

Hero feedback: 'less mistakes, spawning the rights agents, and the actual system we built working in full for once'. Root cause = discipline gap, not capability gap. System has debug/reviewer/Explore agents + triggers rule but no enforcement mechanism. Candidate for Bruce proposal: pre-commit hook or stop-hook check that fails if deploying code without a reviewer pass.

Many preventable bugs shipped and fixed mid-session: [object Object] in DB, subrequest cap blowout requiring chunking refactor, stale archive flood needing purge, invisible checkboxes requiring redeploy, non-clickable cards requiring redeploy. Zero subagents spawned in a 4hr build. → 6 lessons saved
All 3 fix attempts were caused by not verifying external dependencies before coding. Updated code-accuracy.md rule.

Hero had to manually catch 3 inline loop failures: agent deployment summary not presented, surprises not logged inline, fixes not applied inline. The rules existed but were not followed. This is the 2nd+ session where Hero catches what the system should catch automatically.

3 deploy failures from unverified external dependencies (model ID, RSS URLs, JSON parsing). Hero caught 3 inline loop failures (agent summary, surprise logging, inline fixes not triggered automatically). → 4 lessons saved

Stop hook caught 4 unverified-completion claims; pretooluse hook blocked 8 edits until I verified functions/endpoints/URLs — discipline slowed momentum but prevented fabricated output → 3 lessons saved
3 accuracy violations caught by stop hook and Hero directly. Both subagent calls returned empty. Verify-before-ship repeated from prior session. → 5 lessons saved
Vercel GitHub auto-deploy has silently broken 3-4 times across sessions — git push succeeds but Vercel webhook does not fire, leaving live dashboard stale while script claims success

Session-briefing hook does not inject durable corrections — facts Hero has had to repeat across multiple sessions. Two recurring examples: (1) Bruce is /bruce slash command not cron, (2) Vercel GitHub auto-deploy is broken, use CLI. Memory files for both exist but are not auto-loaded at session start, so each new session begins with a stale mental model until Hero corrects it. This is the core OS problem — recurring corrections should be prevented by injection, not re-learned by correction.

Claimed Bruce 3 AM cron would populate evals AND that Vercel auto-deploy would pick up git push — both wrong, corrected by Hero. Root cause: stale mental model at session start + no enforcement of verification-before-claim. Fixed systemically via Stop hook check 7 + session-briefing pinned corrections. → 6 lessons saved
B3 head -40 → head -160
Create infra agent
Library reorganization baseline — measure against this in 2 weeks
selfImprovement.context: Post-reorganization system metrics: CLAUDE.md=46 lines, rules=6 files, global_memory=92 files, mirror_memory=18 files, MEMORY_index=96 lines, hooks=5, skills=34, hook_catches_baseline=11, developer_eval=4.8, cos_eval=4.6

selfImprovement.text: Stop hook caught unverified research claims twice; stale Mac Mini memory required manual correction → 2 lessons saved
Add Multi-Option Recommendations micro-rule to chief-of-staff.md
Increase b3-skill-gaps.sh agent snapshot from 40 to 160 lines
Merge 4 project_agent_* files into project_agent_os.md
Add post-eval audit to b1 — detect silent canary eval failures
Add TC6 adversarial rule compliance eval to developer, replace TC2 canary
Add evidence age filtering to B3 and C0 — prevent stale evidence proposals

Stop hook infinite loop (fixed). PreToolUse blocked new file imports (workaround: Write instead of Edit). Bash 3.2 lacks associative arrays (rewrote with awk). Mac Mini username mismatch (sed fix). → 4 lessons saved
Multiple git rebase conflicts from two machines pushing to same repo → 4 lessons saved
SSH setup failed after 20+ minutes. Dashboard staleness not auto-detected — Hero caught it manually. → 2 lessons saved
Browser cache caused repeated stale dashboard views. SSH setup failed. Explore subagents returned empty summaries twice. → 6 lessons saved
Eval scoring regex produces score=0 for all responses, causing false RED system status
Phase B2 skill gap detection has never worked — multi-line JSON always fails to parse
feedback_electron and feedback_xano clusters consolidated into reference docs
Developer TC1 regression was eval setup issue — canary needs project context, switched to TC2
Developer agent adds unsolicited type hints/docstrings on new code — rule only guarded existing code
TC2 baseline missing from baselines.json — deltas compared against TC1 fallback
Proposal IDs collide when Bruce runs twice in one day — renumber from max existing
Skill gap detection cant see agent files — re-flags addressed gaps as open
Stop hook infinite loop: evaluates full transcript history, re-flags corrected historical violation on every new response. Hook should scope evaluation to current response only.
Stop hook evaluated full transcript instead of latest response — caused infinite rejection loop after a corrected violation. Self-corrected by scoping hook prompt to latest response only.
Stop hook entered infinite loop re-flagging corrected violations — scoped to latest response. PreToolUse hook blocked edits for newly created files. → 0 lessons saved
4 PreToolUse hook catches on developer (API verification), 5 Stop hook catches on chief-of-staff (false certainty) → 3 lessons saved
LinkedIn returns 999 on WebFetch — asked for screenshots instead → 3 lessons saved

nightShift.briefingContent:
# Night Shift — 2026-04-16

## Status: YELLOW

## Key Findings
- WATCH: Friction Rate — 3 fix_attempts in 14 days (red line at 6)

## What Hero Worked On (7-day)
- cloudflare-workers: 4 sessions
- css: 4 sessions
- d1: 4 sessions
- nextjs: 4 sessions
- agents: 3 sessions

## Agent Usage (7-day)
- developer: 2 sessions
- explore: 2 sessions
- research: 2 sessions
- reviewer: 2 sessions
- chief-of-staff: 1 sessions

## Eval Canaries
| Agent | Score | Baseline | Delta | Status |
|-------|-------|----------|-------|--------|
| chief-of-staff | 4.6 | 5.0 | -0.4 | ok |

## Skill Gaps
None detected

## Memory Health
87 total files, 0 consolidation candidates

## Friction Trends (7-day)
- fix_attempts: 3
- escalations: 0
- hook_catches: 0
- re_explanations: 0
- patterns_detected: 4

## Proposals
Now I have everything I need. I can see: P-2026-04-16-001 already exists in the ledger (from a prior targeted review), so tonight's proposals start at 002. The D0 eval table bug is confirmed — the `while IFS= read` pipe loop writes to a temp file, but the issue is likely the `read_events` empty-phase filter behaving differently in D0's context vs C0's. Let me write the proposals.

---

# Proposals — 2026-04-16

---

## P-2026-04-16-002
**Category:** `regression`

**Problem:** chief-of-staff TC1 scored **4.6 vs. baseline 5.0 (−0.4)**. Two criteria were docked:

- **C2 (inter-option dependencies):** *"strategic filter is strong but inter-option dependencies (e.g., T&C as legal blocker vs. Wrapped as retention lever) require contextual judgment the agent handles well but not always with full nuance."*
- **C4 (opportunity cost):** *"opportunity cost language is in the system prompt but tends to be brief rather than exhaustive."*

The root cause for C4 is directly traceable to the agent's own system prompt. The "Multi-Option Recommendations" section includes:

```
- Keep it brief — one line per dimension is enough
```

This instruction correctly prevents rambling, but it also caps the depth of opportunity cost analysis at a single line per option — which is exactly what C4 penalizes. The agent is following the instruction faithfully; the instruction is what's wrong.

The root cause for C2 is related: the same section lists "flag if options interact: mutually exclusive, sequentially dependent, or if one forecloses another" but gives no heuristic for *when to elevate* a dependency above mere enumeration. T&C-as-legal-blocker is categorically different from Wrapped-as-retention-lever — one forecloses the timeline for the other — but the current phrasing treats both as list items of equal weight.

**Solution:** Edit `/Users/reggie/.claude/agents/chief-of-staff.md`, in the `## Multi-Option Recommendations` section. Replace:

```markdown
- Keep it brief — one line per dimension is enough
```

With:

```markdown
- Opportunity cost: write enough to make the tradeoff feel concrete, not just named. One vivid sentence beats five vague bullets.
- If one option is a hard blocker or prerequisite for another, say so at the top — before comparing merit. Dependency framing is not a footnote.
```

**Impact:** C4 recovers because the brevity ceiling is removed while the adjacent "Direct and concise" communication style rule still prevents bloat. C2 recovers because the agent now has an explicit priority rule: blockers are surfaced before merit ranking. Expect TC1 to return to 5.0 on next eval run.

**Risk:** Removing "keep it brief" from this section could make multi-option responses longer. Mitigation: the `## Communication Style` section still reads *"Direct and concise. No filler, no preamble."* — the guardrail exists at the style layer, not just the options layer. Net effect should be more depth, not more words.

---

## P-2026-04-16-003
**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem:** Tonight's D0 briefing shows an **empty Eval Canaries table** despite B1 having produced a confirmed chief-of-staff TC1 score (4.6 — regression, delta −0.4). The same eval data reached C0 successfully (`ALL_EVALS=$(read_events "" "eval" | jq -s '.')`), so the events exist in `events.jsonl` when D0 runs. The table is empty because D0's eval rendering logic fails silently.

Inspecting `/Users/reggie/.claude/night-shift/phases/d0-briefing.sh`: the eval table block pipes `read_events "" "eval"` into a `while IFS= read -r line; do ... done > "$OUT_DIR/.eval_table.tmp"` pattern. The most likely failure: the `read_events` invocation inside a pipe subshell produces no output when the empty-string phase filter (`""`) behaves differently under D0's shell environment than under C0's (e.g., due to IFS differences, quoting, or function-sourcing order). If the pipe produces no lines, the while loop body never executes, the temp file is empty, and `EVAL_TABLE=""` — causing the table to silently render blank.

The consequence: Hero's morning briefing — the primary situational awareness artifact — **shows no regression flag** for tonight's chief-of-staff eval. The regression only becomes visible by reading proposals separately.

**Solution:** Replace the pipe-into-while pattern with a direct jq invocation in `/Users/reggie/.claude/night-shift/phases/d0-briefing.sh`:

```bash
# Replace the existing eval table block with:
EVAL_ROWS=$(grep '"type":"eval"' "$EVENTS_FILE" 2>/dev/null | \
  jq -rs '[.[] | select(.source != "dynamic")] |
    if length == 0 then "| — | — | — | — | no evals this run |"
    else .[] | "| \(.agent) | \(.score) | \(.baseline) | \(.delta) | \(
      if (.delta // 0) < -0.5 then "⚠️ DEGRADED"
      elif (.delta // 0) < 0 then "watch"
      else "ok" end) |"
    end' 2>/dev/null || echo "| — | — | — | — | parse error |")
```

Using `grep '"type":"eval"'` directly on `$EVENTS_FILE` bypasses the `read_events` function and the subshell pipe entirely — the same approach that makes C0's `ALL_EVALS` reliable. The `select(.source != "dynamic")` preserves the existing filter for dynamic evals.

**Impact:** Hero's morning briefing correctly shows the chief-of-staff regression (⚠️ watch) starting tomorrow. The empty-table false confidence is eliminated. Any future eval regression is visible at first glance without cross-referencing `proposals.md`.

**Risk:** Direct `grep` on `$EVENTS_FILE` assumes the events file path variable is defined in D0's scope — which it is (sourced from `lib/common.sh`). The jq inline logic is slightly more complex but testable with a single jq command. The fallback string ("parse error") makes any new failure visible rather than silent.

---

## P-2026-04-16-004
**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem:** Tonight's A1 metrics report **`capability_gaps_7d: 8`** — 8 instances in the past 7 days where an agent explicitly could not fulfill a request. Tonight's B3 analysis reported **"Skill Gaps: None detected."** These two data points are contradictory and the contradiction is architectural.

B3 (`b3-skill-gaps.sh`) infers capability gaps by running an LLM over session digest summaries — qualitative text describing what happened in each session. If an agent's limitation wasn't described in the digest in B3-recognizable terms, the gap goes undetected. The 8 `capability_gap` events come from a separate source: real-time hook events logged during sessions when an agent explicitly declined or punted a task. B3 never reads these events — it only reads digests.

The result: 8 concrete "I can't do that" moments are invisible to Bruce's analysis every time B3 produces silence. Over 7 days, capability gaps are accumulating at >1/day, but Bruce proposes nothing because B3 sees nothing.

**Solution:** Add a pre-analysis block to `/Users/reggie/.claude/night-shift/phases/b3-skill-gaps.sh` that reads raw `capability_gap` events before the gap analysis prompt runs:

```bash
# Add before the GAP_PROMPT construction:
RAW_GAPS=$(grep '"type":"capability_gap"' "$EVENTS_FILE" 2>/dev/null | \
  jq -rs 'if length == 0 then ""
    else "## Raw Capability Gap Events (7d — from session hooks)\n" +
      (map("- \(.date // .timestamp // "unknown"): \(.text // .value // "no detail")") | join("\n"))
    end' 2>/dev/null || echo "")
```

Then inject `$RAW_GAPS` into the `GAP_PROMPT` after the digest summary section, with the instruction:

```
If Raw Capability Gap Events are present, treat each as confirmed evidence of a gap.
Cluster by domain if 2+ events share a pattern. A cluster of 3+ = HIGH severity regardless of digest mentions.
```

**Impact:** The 8 capability gap events get analyzed on their own terms — not inferred from digests that may or may not mention them. B3 gains a direct evidence channel. Tonight's "None detected" would instead surface the gap domains, enabling proposals calibrated to what Hero actually encountered. The `capability_gaps_7d` metric becomes actionable, not just decorative.

**Risk:** Capability gap event text quality varies — some hooks log terse messages like `"agent declined: out of scope"` with no domain signal. In that case, B3's LLM analysis would produce weak findings. But weak findings are surfaced (and improvable) rather than suppressed. If event text is consistently poor, a follow-on proposal can improve the hook's logging format.

---

## P-2026-04-16-005
**Category:** `eval_evolution`

**Problem:** The chief-of-staff has **one canary eval: TC1 (Feature Prioritization)**. Tonight TC1 scored 4.6, with C2 (inter-option dependencies) and C4 (opportunity cost depth) as the specific failure modes. TC1 can detect *that* a regression occurred, but its three-option prioritization prompt doesn't isolate inter-option dependency reasoning — the agent can score 4.6 even when dependency handling is only superficially addressed.

TC4 (**Cross-Dimensional Decision** — "Should I connect to Apple Health?") is already fully written in `/Users/reggie/.claude/agents/evals/chief-of-staff-evals.md` and tests the exact capability that regressed tonight:

| TC4 Criterion | What It Tests | Tonight's Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| C1 | Multi-dimensional analysis (product, technical, behavioral, business) | Same as TC1-C2 (contextual judgment) |
| C2 | Validates against Mirror's behavioral thesis before endorsing | Strategic filter under a seductive idea |
| C4 | Flags costs and dependencies (Apple Dev Program, HealthKit, App Store review) | Direct analog to C4 opportunity cost depth |
| C5 | Grounds in evidence, questions single-user request as sufficient | Reality-check instinct |

TC4 is harder than TC1: it involves specific technical barriers (HealthKit, Apple Developer Program costs), behavioral science framing (does sleep-spending correlation serve Mirror's loop?), and evidence discipline (one user ≠ validated need). TC1 can pass at 4.6 while TC4 surfaces a 3.5 — meaning TC4 would have caught tonight's regression more sharply.

**Staleness check:** TC4 is an existing eval test case (not a new agent), written against active Mirror App context. No stale-evidence concern applies.

**Solution:**
1. Add TC4 as a second chief-of-staff canary in `/Users/reggie/.claude/night-shift/config.json`. If the config supports only one `prompt_id` per agent, add a second entry:
   ```json
   "chief-of-staff-tc4": {
     "eval_file": "~/.claude/agents/evals/chief-of-staff-evals.md",
     "prompt_id": "TC4",
     "agent": "chief-of-staff"
   }
   ```
2. Set the TC4 baseline from the **first scored run** (do not pre-set to 5.0). Expected first-run score: 4.0–4.5, reflecting that HealthKit-specific knowledge and evidence-discipline criteria are genuinely harder.
3. Add a note in `baselines.json` once baseline is set: *"TC4 added 2026-04-16 — harder eval targeting inter-option dependency and behavioral thesis validation."*

**Impact:** The chief-of-staff eval suite gains a stress test that surfaces the exact failure modes tonight's regression revealed. The `consecutive_all_pass` counter (currently 3) resets when TC4 first runs — correct behavior for introducing a harder canary. Future regressions in dependency reasoning are detectable before TC1 alone shows a delta.

**Risk:** Running two chief-of-staff evals per night doubles B1 token cost for that agent. Acceptable: chief-of-staff is the most strategically critical agent in the roster and has an active regression. If budget is a constraint, TC4 can run on alternating nights by adding a day-parity check (`[ $(( $(date +%d) % 2 )) -eq 0 ]`) in the B1 phase.

## Proposal Track Record
32 total, 16 accepted, 6 pending

## Benchmarks
| Benchmark | Status | Detail |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Friction Rate | WATCH | 3 fix_attempts (14d) |
| Proposal Hit Rate | 59% | 16/27 accepted |
| Eval Signal | OK | 3 consecutive all-pass runs |

## Action Required: NO
